claim: Decreasing tensile temperature reduces stacking fault energy (SFE), which promotes planar glide of dislocations and leads to a larger fraction of low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs) at lower temperature.
evidence_text: The decreased tensile temperature, which is corresponding to reduced stacking fault energy (SFE), promotes the planar glide of dislocations. This led to larger fraction of low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs) at lower tensile temperature.

claim: Fig. 1 presents the EBSD IPF map (a) and phase map (b) of the initial steel, and the XRD patterns (c) of the initial steel and after holding at −196 °C for 30 min, confirming a fully austenitic structure.
evidence_text: Fig. 1. EBSD IPF map (a) and phase map (b) of initial steel; (c) XRD pattern of the initial steel and after holding at − 1 9 6 °C for 30 min.

claim: The steel was melted and cast in a 50 kg vacuum induction furnace, air cooled to RT, reheated to 1250 °C and held for 2 h for homogenization, then forged from 1100 °C to 950 °C into round bars of 60 mm diameter and furnace cooled to RT.
evidence_text: The experimental steel was melted and cast in a 50 kg vacuum induction furnace and then air cooled to RT. The ingot was then reheated to 1250 ~ { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } and held for 2 h for homogenization. Subsequently, the ingot was forged into round bars with a diameter of 60 mm followed by cooling to RT in the furnace. The starting and finishing forging temperatures were 1100 and 950 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , respectively.

claim: The initial forged microstructure was analyzed by EBSD and XRD, confirming a fully austenitic phase.
evidence_text: The initial microstructure of the forged steel was analyzed using electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) on an Apreo S HiVac scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a SmartLab SE X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation...

claim: Tensile tests were performed at −196 °C (liquid nitrogen), −77 °C (dry ice-alcohol mixture), and RT at a strain rate of 0.001 s⁻¹ with an extensometer; three samples per condition were used.
evidence_text: Tensile tests were carried out on a YYF-50 tensile tester until fracture. Liquid nitrogen ( - 196 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ) and a dry ice-alcohol mixture ( - 77 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ) were used as cooling medium. ... the adopted strain rate of 0.001 s–1 ... Three tensile samples ... were tested for each condition to get the average values.

claim: Tensile specimens had a parallel length of 32 mm, width of 5 mm, and thickness of 1.5 mm.
evidence_text: Three tensile samples with a parallel length of 32 mm, width of 5 mm and thickness of 1.5 mm were tested for each condition...

claim: Ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and yield strength (YS) increased linearly with decreasing temperature, following UTS = 813.0 – 2.36 T (MPa) and YS = 474.6 – 2.05 T (MPa) with T in °C; at −196 °C, UTS ≈ 1300 MPa and YS ≈ 900 MPa, while at RT (0 °C in the plot) UTS ≈ 800 MPa and YS ≈ 400 MPa.
evidence_text: The chart includes two data series: ... and an 'UTS=813.0-2.36*T' and 'YS=474.6-2.05*T' markers. The chart is labeled '(c)' in the top-left corner. ... | Tensile temperature (°C) | TE Elongation (%) | UE Elongation (%) | UTS Strength (MPa) | YS Strength (MPa) | | -200 | 68 | 47 | 1300 | 900 | | -100 | 58 | 45 | 1000 | 650 | | 0 | 32 | 28 | 800 | 400 |

claim: At an engineering strain of 10%, the engineering stress was ~1000 MPa at −196 °C, ~800 MPa at −77 °C, and ~600 MPa at RT.
The work hardening rate (dσ_true/dε_true) decreased with increasing true strain, e.g., from ~2400 at true strain 0.1 to ~2300 at true strain 0.2.

After tensile deformation, the low-angle grain boundary (LAGB, 2–15°) fraction decreased with increasing temperature: 70.2% at −196 °C, 63.0% at −77 °C, and 56.0% at RT; the high-angle grain boundary (HAGB, >15°) fractions were 29.8%, 37.0%, and 44.0%, respectively.
Charpy impact tests were performed at −196 °C, −77 °C, and RT using V-notch specimens of 10 × 10 × 55 mm³ with a 2 mm deep notch.
evidence_text: Charpy impact experiments were conducted at −196 °C, −77 °C and RT ... samples with the size of 10 × 10 × 55 mm with a 2 mm depth V-notch were used...

claim: Fracture surfaces after tensile tests were examined by Nova 400 Nano SEM; after Charpy impact tests, fracture surfaces were examined by VHX-5000 3D microscope and SEM, and cross-sections near the fracture were prepared by argon ion-beam polishing and analyzed by EBSD with a step size of 0.8 μm.
evidence_text: The fracture surface after tensile tests was observed by a Nova 400 Nano SEM. ... fracture surface after the Charpy impact tests was observed by VHX-5000 ultra-depth of field three-dimensional (3D) microscope and SEM. ... argon ionbeam cross-section polishing ... Then the microstructure was analyzed by EBSD. The step size for EBSD analyses was 0.8 μm.

claim: Fig. 3 presents EBSD analyses of tensile-deformed steel: IPF maps (a–c), grain boundary maps with LAGB/HAGB fractions (d–f), and GND distribution maps superimposed on GB maps for the boxed regions in (d–f).
evidence_text: Fig. 3. EBSD analyses for the experimental steel deformed at different temperature: (a,d,g) − 196 ◦C; (b,e,h) − 77 ◦C; (c,f,i) RT. (a–c) IPF maps; (d–f) GB maps; (g–i) GND distribution maps superposed with GB maps...

claim: The experimental steel has an estimated density of 7.078 g/cm³ and a measured density of 7.114 g/cm³, calculated using a composition-based equation.
evidence_text: The estimated density is 7.078 g/cm3 and the measured density is 7.114 g/cm3.

claim: XRD patterns of as-forged and -196 °C treated samples confirm a fully austenitic initial microstructure and show no isothermal martensite at -196 °C, indicating very stable austenite.
evidence_text: The XRD patterns of as-forged steel and as-treated at -196 °C shown in Fig. 1c also suggest that the initial microstructure is fully austenitic and no isothermal martensite forms at -196 °C, indicating a very stable austenite.

claim: At -77 °C and -196 °C the work hardening rate initially drops sharply then becomes near-linear with strain, whereas at RT it decreases continuously; the work hardening rate increases with decreasing temperature, indicating a negative temperature dependence of strength.
evidence_text: the experimental steels deformed at −77 and −196 °C show a near-linear work hardening rate with strain, but a continuous decrease is observed at RT. ... as the deformation temperature decreases, the work hardening rate increases, indicating a negative temperature dependence of strength.

claim: After tensile deformation at -196 °C, a small amount of mechanical twins form with the twinning plane on {111} and along the <112> direction in a grain oriented near the <110> zone axis, confirmed by TEM.
evidence_text: When deformation temperature decreases to −196 °C, the decreasing SFE leads to small amount of mechanical twins formed during tensile deformation, as marked with arrows in Fig. 3a. ... It is seen that the mechanical twinning plane formed along <112> crystal direction on {111} plane in the case of the grain oriented near <110> zone axis.

claim: Tensile deformation at -77 °C produces no mechanical twins but microbands are observed; SAED indicates these microbands lie on {111} planes and exhibit characteristics of planar glide.
evidence_text: Compared with the sample deformed at −196 °C, no mechanical twins but microbands are observed in the sample deformed at −77 °C, as shown in Fig. 4b. ... The corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern indicates that these bands are orientated along {111} planes.

claim: The fraction of LAGBs dramatically increases with decreasing deformation temperature and LAGBs mainly concentrate in finer austenite grains.
evidence_text: the fraction of LAGBs dramatically increases with the decreasing temperature. Additionally, the LAGBs mainly concentrate at these austenite grain with finer grain size.

claim: At -196 °C the impact fracture surface exhibits dimples, large dimples and tear edges, while at -77 °C and RT the fracture surface shows dimples.
evidence_text: (d) dimples large dimples tear edges 100 µm (e) dimples 20 µm (f) dimples 20 µm

claim: The Charpy impact absorbed energy (W_all) of the experimental steel is about 327 J at room temperature, 277 J at -77 °C, and 188 J at -196 °C.
evidence_text: The W_all at RT is about 327 J. As the test temperatures decreases to -77 and -196 °C, the W_all reduces to 277 and 188 J, respectively.

claim: Almost no fibrous zone is observed on the sample tested at -196 °C, while a larger fibrous zone appears at -77 °C and RT, indicating better toughness at -77 °C and RT.
evidence_text: Almost no fibrous zone can be observed on the sample tested at -196 °C, while a larger fibrous zone appears at -77 °C and RT, suggesting that the experimental steel exhibits a better toughness at -77 °C and RT.

claim: The 3D topography maps in Fig. 6a–c show that the fracture surface of the impact sample tested at -196 °C appeared significantly flatter compared to those at -77 °C and RT.
evidence_text: The inserted 3D topography maps in Fig. 6a-c suggest that the fracture surface of the impact sample at -196 °C appeared significantly flatter compared to others.

claim: At -196 °C, the impact fracture surface contains high-density large dimples and tear edges, indicating a combination of ductile and brittle fracture with quasi-cleavage crack tendency, consistent with the low absorbed energy of 188 J.
evidence_text: At -196 °C, the tested sample displayed a combination of ductile and brittle fracture. The fracture surfaces contain high-density large dimples and tear edges, as marked in Fig. 6d. These features indicate a tendency to be quasicleavage cracks [30], which is consistent with the relatively low W_all of 188 J.

claim: The fracture morphology at -77 °C and RT demonstrates more obvious ductile fracture, consistent with the higher Charpy absorbed energy at these temperatures.
evidence_text: the fracture morphology characteristics for the sample tested at -77 °C and RT demonstrate a more obvious ductile fracture, which is in good agreement with the result that the W_all is higher at -77 °C and RT.

claim: The stacking fault energy (SFE) of the experimental steel is estimated to be 32 mJ/m² at -196 °C, 40 mJ/m² at -77 °C, and 61 mJ/m² at room temperature, based on thermodynamic calculation.
evidence_text: The SFE of the experimental steel at -196 °C, -77 °C and RT is estimated to be 32, 40 and 61 mJ/m2, respectively.

claim: For high Mn steels, mechanical twinning is the main deformation mechanism when the stacking fault energy is between 18 and 35 mJ/m²; outside this range, either DIMT or dislocation planar glide dominates.
evidence_text: Normally, for high Mn steels, when the SFE is between 18 and 35 mJ/m2 [42], mechanical twinning is the main deformation mechanism. Outside this range, DIMT and dislocation planar glide dominate deformation mechanism [42].

claim: The stacking fault energy of the experimental steel at RT and -77 °C is high enough to suppress deformation-induced martensitic transformation and mechanical twinning, while at -196 °C the SFE of 32 mJ/m² can suppress DIMT but enable mechanical twinning.
evidence_text: The SFE of the experimental steel at RT and -77 °C is high enough that DIMT and mechanical twinning can be validly suppressed. When the deformation temperature decreases to -196 °C, a SFE of 32 mJ/m2 could still totally suppress DIMT but enable the occurrence of mechanical twinning.

claim: As deformation temperature decreases from RT to -196 °C, the decreasing stacking fault energy promotes dislocation planar glide, resulting in both increased tensile strength and elongation.
evidence_text: Therefore, as deformation temperature decreases from RT to -196 °C, the decreasing SFE promotes dislocation planar glide, resulting in the increasing strength and elongation (Fig. 2c).

claim: The work hardening behavior shows two stages with exponents n1 (low strain) and n2 (high strain); n1 increases with decreasing temperature due to reduced stacking fault energy promoting planar glide and suppressing dynamic recovery.
evidence_text: It can be observed that the work hardening behavior of the experimental steels can be divided into two stages, where n1 represents the slope in low strain region and n2 represents the slope in high strain region. ... At lower temperatures, the reduced SFE not only contributes to the planarity of the dislocation glide but also hampered cross-slip, resulting in reduced dynamic recovery and increased strain hardening capability. Consequently, the work hardening exponent n1 increases with decreasing temperature.

claim: At higher strains, extensive deformation induces high-density slip bands (microbands) that impede dislocation motion, and at -196 °C mechanical twins also contribute to the improved work hardening.
evidence_text: At higher strain, extensive deformation induces a high density of slip bands, which are commonly referred to as microbands in austenitic low-density steels [27]. These microbands serve as barriers that impede dislocation motion, significantly enhancing the work hardening rate [44]. Besides, the presence of mechanical twins also plays an important role in the improved work hardening at -196 °C.

claim: The work hardening behavior of the experimental steel can be divided into two stages, with n1 representing the slope in the low strain region and n2 representing the slope in the high strain region.
evidence_text: the work hardening behavior of the experimental steels can be divided into two stages, where n represents the slope in low strain region and n2 represents the slope in high strain region.

claim: At lower temperatures, reduced stacking fault energy enhances planar dislocation glide and hampers cross-slip, reducing dynamic recovery and increasing the work hardening exponent n1.
evidence_text: At lower temperatures, the reduced SFE not only contributes to the planarity of the dislocation glide but also hampered cross-slip, resulting in reduced dynamic recovery and increased strain hardening capability. Consequently, the work hardening exponent n1 increases with decreasing temperature.

claim: At higher strains, extensive deformation induces microbands that act as barriers impeding dislocation motion and significantly enhance the work hardening rate; at lower temperatures, a higher n2 value indicates a more substantial strengthening effect from microbands.
evidence_text: At higher strain, extensive deformation induces a high density of slip bands, which are commonly referred to as microbands in austenitic low-density steels. These microbands serve as barriers that impede dislocation motion, significantly enhancing the work hardening rate. At lower temperatures, a higher n value indicates a more substantial strengthening effect from microbands.

claim: The presence of mechanical twins also plays an important role in the improved work hardening at -196 °C.
evidence_text: Besides, the presence of mechanical twins also plays an important role in the improved work hardening at −196 °C.

claim: The general yield load Fgy shows a negative dependence on temperature, consistent with the increase in yield strength with decreasing temperature; the maximum load Fm increases with decreasing temperature; crack initiation load Fin is larger at lower temperatures, indicating greater crack initiation resistance.
evidence_text: It can be found from Fig. 9d that the change of Fgy shows a negative dependence of temperature, which is in good agreement with the increase in YS with the decreasing temperature. In addition, as temperature decreases, Fm increases, indicating that the experimental steel could withstand larger impact load at lower temperatures. A larger Fin at lower temperature indicates that it is more difficult for crack initiation.

claim: The Fiu/Fm ratio is larger at lower temperature, reflecting rapid transition from stable to unstable crack propagation; rebound compliance RC is larger at lower temperature, indicating faster unstable crack growth.
evidence_text: A larger Fiu/Fm at lower temperature suggests that cracks rapidly propagate from stable to unstable regime. Rebound compliance (RC) ... is larger at lower temperature, which is a hint that unstable cracks propagate more rapidly at lower temperature.

claim: The crack initiation energy Winitiation at -196 °C, -77 °C and RT is 112, 68 and 57 J, respectively, increasing with decreasing temperature; both Wstable and Wunstable decrease with decreasing temperature, indicating weaker crack propagation resistance at low temperature.
evidence_text: The Winitiation at -196 °C, -77 °C and RT is 112, 68 and 57 J, respectively. The increasing Winitiation with decreasing temperature reveals that the resistance of the experimental steel to crack initiation is stronger at lower temperature. ... as temperature decrease, obvious decreases in both Wstable and Wunstable can be observed, implying that the resistance to crack propagation is weaker at lower temperature.

claim: EBSD analysis of cross-sections near fracture surface after impact at -196 °C and RT shows primary crack paths with a zigzag shape and predominantly transgranular fracture; misorientation across the crack in grain A is less than 15°, confirming transgranular fracture.
evidence_text: For samples tested at −196 °C and RT, the primary crack propagation paths at the fracture surface exhibit a zigzag shape. Most regions exhibit the characteristic of transgranular fracture. Misorientation analysis along the marked line for grain A is presented in Fig. 10c. The misorientation difference between the regions on both sides of the crack is less than 15°, implying that the two regions are in the same grain before the crack occurred. This suggests that transgranular fracture occurred in this region.

claim: Secondary cracks are observed near the primary crack after impact testing at RT; they release stress concentration at the primary crack tip, delay fracture, and contribute to higher crack propagation energy and better toughness.
evidence_text: secondary cracks present in the sample tested at RT, which is different from that at −196 °C. The presence of secondary cracks release stress concentration at the tip of the primary cracks, making stress redistribute within material. Hence, impact fracture is delayed, contributing to a better toughness.

claim: SEM fractography (Fig. 11) shows that only few regions exhibit planar glide features after impact at -196 °C, while planar glide becomes more pronounced as temperature increases; these planar glide features retard crack propagation and increase toughness, explaining the weaker crack propagation resistance at low temperature.
evidence_text: Noted that only few regions experienced planar glide during impact deformation at −196 °C, as shown in Fig. 11a. As temperature increases, planar glide becomes more pronounced. These planar glide features could retard the propagation of crack, thus increasing toughness.

claim: Despite higher crack initiation energy, the overall absorbed impact energy (Wall) is lowest at -196 °C because rapid unstable crack propagation leads to lower crack propagation energy.
evidence_text: Because the rapid unstable crack propagation, the Wall is the lowest at -196 °C.
